name: Petra
summary: Petra is an ancient city carved into rose-red sandstone cliffs in southern Jordan, established as the capital of the Nabataean Kingdom around 312 BC. The city's sophisticated water management system and monumental architecture made it one of the wealthiest trading hubs of the ancient world. Lost to the Western world for centuries, it was rediscovered in 1812 and is now Jordan's most visited tourist attraction.

timeline.title: Nabataean Capital Established
timeline.description: The Nabataeans establish Petra as their capital, taking advantage of its strategic location at the crossroads of trade routes connecting Arabia, Egypt, and the Mediterranean. The city is carved from the narrow canyon known as the Siq, which provides natural defense against invasion. The Nabataeans develop an ingenious water management system of cisterns, channels, and dams that allows the desert city to support a population of 20,000 to 30,000 people.

timeline.title: Roman Annexation
timeline.description: Emperor Trajan annexes the Nabataean Kingdom into the Roman Empire, making Petra the capital of the new province of Arabia Petraea. The Romans add their own architectural elements including colonnaded streets, a grand triumphal arch, and a theater expanded to seat 8,500 spectators. Despite Roman rule, Petra continues to flourish as a major trading center, particularly for incense, spices, and silk.

timeline.title: Earthquake and Decline
timeline.description: A devastating earthquake strikes Petra, destroying many of its buildings and severely damaging its water management system. The earthquake, combined with shifting trade routes that increasingly favor sea transport, accelerates Petra's decline as a commercial hub. The city gradually shrinks, and by the end of the Byzantine period, it is reduced to a small settlement. Many of its grand structures are left abandoned and begin to be reclaimed by the desert.

timeline.title: Rediscovery by Johann Ludwig Burckhardt
timeline.description: Swiss explorer Johann Ludwig Burckhardt becomes the first European in modern times to see Petra, disguising himself as an Arab pilgrim to gain access through the narrow Siq canyon. He identifies the famous Treasury (Al-Khazneh) and documents his findings, reigniting European interest in the lost city. Burckhardt's accounts inspire a wave of explorers, artists, and eventually archaeologists to travel to southern Jordan.

timeline.title: First Archaeological Excavations
timeline.description: British archaeologists Agnes Conway and George Horsfield conduct the first scientific archaeological excavations at Petra, systematically documenting the site's monuments and uncovering artifacts from Nabataean, Roman, and Byzantine periods. Their work establishes Petra's significance beyond its spectacular facades, revealing a complex city with advanced engineering, vibrant trade networks, and rich cultural traditions spanning over a millennium.

timeline.title: UNESCO World Heritage Designation
timeline.description: Petra is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognized as one of the most precious cultural properties of humanity's cultural heritage. The designation highlights Petra's unique blend of Eastern traditions with Hellenistic architecture and its outstanding universal value as a masterwork of human creative genius. International conservation efforts are launched to preserve the sandstone monuments from erosion and weathering.

timeline.title: New7Wonders of the World
timeline.description: Petra is named one of the New Seven Wonders of the World in a global poll, joining the Great Wall of China, Christ the Redeemer, Machu Picchu, Chichen Itza, the Roman Colosseum, and the Taj Mahal. The announcement dramatically increases Petra's international profile and visitor numbers, which reach over one million annually. Jordan invests heavily in infrastructure and conservation to manage the tourism boom.

images.caption: The Treasury (Al-Khazneh) emerging through the narrow Siq canyon, carved from rose-red sandstone
The Siq gorge, the narrow 1.2 kilometer canyon that serves as the main entrance to Petra
The Roman-style theater at Petra, originally carved by the Nabataeans and later expanded by the Romans to seat 8,500
Ad-Deir (the Monastery), one of Petra's largest monuments, standing 48 meters high and 47 meters wide
